1:
The parable of the marriage feast draws out the chapter's richest patristic allegory, and the witnesses divide chiefly over how far to press its details. The Latin fathers read every element typologically. Gregory the Great takes the marriage as God the Father joining the Son to human nature in the Virgin's womb — guarding carefully that Christ is "of two natures" but not "compounded of two persons" — and reads the oxen as the fathers of the Old Testament, the fatlings as those of the New, raised "by the wing of contemplation"; the wedding garment, he says (with Augustine), is nothing but charity, which Christ "had upon Him when He came to espouse the Church." Augustine adds that the man without the garment "goes seeking his own, and not the Bridegroom's honour."

The Antiochene Chrysostom and Pseudo-Chrysostom stress instead the historical message: the parable proclaims "the casting out of the Jews and the calling of the Gentiles," and "indicates the strictness of the life required" — Pseudo-Chrysostom reading the "all things now ready" as the table of Scripture spread out of Law and Prophets. The Alexandrian Origen multiplies senses: the kingdom is like a king in respect of the One who reigns, like a king's son in respect of Him who shares it; the oxen are "the more mighty of the oracles of God," the fatlings "the sweet and pleasant."

The Puritan Henry gives the verse its devotional fullness, hearing in the feast "a heaven upon earth now, and a heaven in heaven shortly," prophesied as the feast of fat things (Isaiah 25:6) and consummated at the marriage of the Lamb (Revelation 19:7). The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott keep to the form: Robertson notes Matthew alone gives this parable, distinct from the supper of Luke 14:16-23, and that the plural gamous denotes the wedding festivities. The Reformed Calvin places it among the discourses that "excited the scribes to the highest pitch of fury." The Wesleyan Wesley keeps it terse — God made the feast "when he brought his first-begotten into the world." So the one structural debate is whether this is Luke's supper relocated; Augustine and Robertson hold them distinct parables, the order itself showing the difference, while Calvin notes that Matthew, unconcerned with strict chronology, set it where it would best expose why the scribes raged.
2:
"The kingdom of heaven is like unto a certain king, which made a marriage for his son." The witnesses agree almost unanimously on the key to the allegory, differing only in emphasis. The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices state the equation plainly: the king is God, the son is Christ, the marriage is the union of Christ with His Church, and the parable as a whole "refers undoubtedly to the rejection of the Jews and the calling of the Gentiles" (Barnes). Ellicott traces the bridal image to the older prophets, who spoke of God as the Bridegroom and Israel as His bride (Isaiah 62:5), and of Israel's idolatry as "the adultery of the faithless wife" (Jeremiah 3:1-4); here, he notes, "the prominent idea is that of the guests," who, so far as they obey and are clothed, are themselves "in their collective unity, the Church which is the bride" (Revelation 19:7-9; Ephesians 5:23-27).

The grammatical-historical Robertson adds a philological note, observing that the plural gamous is common in the papyri for the days-long wedding festivities (seven days in Judges 14:17). Clarke remarks that a rabbinic parable in Bereshith Rabba runs close to this one, "but these rabbinical parables are vastly ennobled by passing through the hands of our Lord," and that the occasion in Luke's parallel was itself a feast.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the double favour the Jews had received — first their election above the nations, then the announcement of their adoption by the prophets — comparing the custom of drawing up a guest-list beforehand and then sending servants to summon those already named: "God elected the Jews in preference to others, as if they had been his familiar friends." The Wesleyan Wesley sums it simply: "So did God, when he brought his first-begotten into the world." Clarke, drawing the lines of the whole parable, identifies the king as God, the son as Christ, the marriage as the incarnation "by taking human nature into union with himself," and the feast as "the economy of the Gospel, during which men are invited to partake of the blessings purchased by the incarnation and death of our blessed Lord." Gill adds that the parable is designed "to express the great love of God the Father," the methods of grace under the gospel dispensation, "and the behaviour of men under it."
3:
"And sent forth his servants to call them that were bidden to the wedding: and they would not come." The witnesses are united on the identity of the servants and the nature of the refusal. The grammatical-historical commentators see in the servants "the aggregate work of the prophets up to the time of the Baptist" (Ellicott), now joined by the first preachers of the gospel. Robertson notes the telling Greek play — kalesai tous keklēmenous, "to call the called" — and that "it was a Jewish custom to invite a second time the already invited" (Esther 5:8; 6:14): the prophets had given the first invitation, and now "the Baptist and Jesus had given the second invitation that the feast was ready."

On the refusal, all the schools fasten on the will. Barnes states the principle that recurs through the whole chapter: "they might have come if they chose, but they would not. So all the difficulty that sinners ever labour under in regard to salvation is in the will. It is a fixed determination not to come and be saved." Robertson, citing Conder, calls this stubborn refusal of the Jewish leaders "The Hebrew Tragedy," and links it to John 1:11, "He came unto his own, and his own received him not."

Gill identifies the servants particularly with "John the Baptist, and the twelve apostles of Christ," the ministers "of God's choosing and ordaining, of his calling and sending," who serve "voluntarily and cheerfully." Ellicott adds that the refusal, which might at first seem dramatically improbable — who would decline so great an honour? — is "natural enough in subjects who were in heart rebellious and disloyal," and that this is "precisely the character which the parable was intended to portray." The summons, he notes, came "in the first instance to those who had long ago been bidden," the Jews who as such had all along been "children of the kingdom." So across Reformed and grammatical voices alike the verse holds two truths in tension: the long, patient antecedent grace that had "bidden" Israel through the prophets, and the settled obstinacy of a will that, fully invited and fully able, simply "would not come."
4:
"Again, he sent forth other servants... Behold, I have prepared my dinner: my oxen and my fatlings are killed." The witnesses agree on the renewed summons and divide gently over the menu. On the servants, the grammatical-historical and Reformed voices see the second sending as the apostles and gospel preachers after the resurrection, sent (with Gill) "any time before the destruction of Jerusalem," and (with Barnes) as God repeating "his message to sinners when they reject it."

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell with characteristic care on the word "dinner." Robertson and Ellicott both note that the Greek ariston is properly the morning meal or breakfast, not the evening deipnon; Robertson traces the shift by which it came to mean a midday meal, "like the French breakfast at noon." On the "oxen and fatlings," Barnes and Clarke explain the fatlings as fat animals, perhaps fatted rams or calves (2 Samuel 6:13; 1 Chronicles 15:26), and Ellicott reads them, "under an imagery which Isaiah had already used" (Isaiah 25:6), as "the spiritual blessings of peace and joy which Christ came to offer."

The Reformed Calvin presses the renewed urgency: the first invitation "includes all the former prophecies, down to the publication of the Gospel," but now "fresh messengers were sent to entreat them to come with haste; for they had been long ago informed as to the time." He notes that the cruelty of the people "grew as the time advanced, and at length spent all its force on Christ and the apostles." The Puritan Henry expands the "all things are ready" into a catalogue of grace — "the Father is ready to accept of us, the Son to intercede for us, the Spirit to sanctify us; pardon is ready, peace is ready, comfort is ready" — and asks, "Is all this ready, and shall we be unready?" Wesley glosses the fatlings simply as "fatted beasts and fowls," and JFB here comments off the main thread, on the king's compassion in the parallel parable of the debtor. The renewed embassy, then, is read by all as God's persistence: when the first messengers were slighted, He did not withdraw the feast but "sent forth other servants," pressing the invitation with fuller proclamation that everything needful for salvation was now provided and ready.
5:
"But they made light of it, and went their ways, one to his farm, another to his merchandise." The witnesses treat this verse as the portrait of careless worldliness, and they are of one mind. The grammatical-historical commentators fix on the Greek amelēsantes, "neglecting, not caring for." Robertson notes the word does not quite say they ridiculed the invitation, "but to neglect an invitation to a wedding feast is a gross discourtesy," and glosses the two pursuits — idion agron (his own field) and emporian (merchandise, the only New Testament instance, from emporos, a travelling merchant). Barnes calls their conduct "an exact representation of the conduct of sinners in regard to the gospel," for whom "the world is of more value than God."

The Puritan Henry makes this the chapter's most searching pastoral note. "Making light of Christ, and of the great salvation wrought out by him, is the damning sin of the world." He distinguishes it from open hostility: "multitudes perish eternally through mere carelessness, who have not any direct aversion, but a prevailing indifference." And he weighs the lawful character of the distractions — the farms and the shops — concluding with the proverb Licitis perimus omnes, "these lawful things undo us, when they are unlawfully managed... whatever we have of the world in our hands, our care must be to keep it out of our hearts."

The Reformed Calvin reads the same lesson with sharper edge: the Jews were "so entirely devoted to the world and to earthly things, that no man found leisure to approach to God." He universalises it — "hardly one person in a hundred prefers the kingdom of God to fading riches" — and observes the cunning by which men "hold out fair pretences," pleading lawful business as an excuse, when in truth "even holy marriage, and fields, and other riches, are so many snares to bind every man more closely to the earth." The Wesleyan Wesley distils it: "One must mind what he has; another, gain what he wants. How many perish by misusing lawful things!" Gill adds that this neglect arises "from loving of the world too much," whether by absence, inattention, or contempt of the gospel's worth.
6:
"And the remnant took his servants, and entreated them spitefully, and slew them." Where the previous verse showed careless neglect, this one shows open malice, and the witnesses mark the distinction sharply. Barnes draws the line most clearly: "a part made light of it, and treated it with silent contempt... the others were not satisfied with that, but showed positive malignity. Some sinners seem to be well satisfied by merely neglecting religion, while others proceed against it with open violence and bitter malice." He adds the motive: men "abuse and calumniate ministers of religion because they hate God, and can in no way else show it so well."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that the Greek word for "entreated spitefully" "implies the wanton infliction of outrage," and that "the parable at this stage looks forward as well as backward," embracing "the sufferings of Christian preachers and martyrs as well as those of the prophets who were sent to Israel." The treatment of the servants, that is, gathers into one the whole history of persecuted messengers, from the slain prophets of the old covenant to the apostles and witnesses of the new.

The Reformed Gill makes the same forward reference concrete, seeing in the seized and imprisoned servants the apostles "quickly after our Lord's ascension, particularly Peter and John," who were laid hold on and detained. He distinguishes, too, the two classes within the invited: some "went to their several worldly callings and occupations of life, and troubled themselves no further about the Messiah," while "others of them were more spiteful and injurious," not merely slighting the message but abusing the messengers. The verse thus completes the indictment begun in the previous one: indifference and violence are two faces of the same rejection, and the parable holds both up to judgment. The Puritan Henry (commenting across these verses) had already named the persecutors as "the scribes, and Pharisees, and chief priests," whose feet should have been beautiful for bringing glad tidings (Nahum 1:15), but who instead treated Christ's messengers "as the offscouring of all things" (1 Corinthians 4:13). The verse thus carries the parable from the careless to the cruel, and the witnesses agree that this open malice, more than mere neglect, is what fills up the measure of guilt and brings down the judgment of the verse that follows.
7:
"But when the king heard thereof, he was wroth: and he sent forth his armies, and destroyed those murderers, and burned up their city." The witnesses are virtually unanimous that this points to the destruction of Jerusalem by the Romans. The Latin father Jerome states it directly: "by His armies we understand those Romans under Vespasian and Titus, who, having slaughtered the inhabitants of Judaea, laid in ashes the faithless city"; and he notes the shift in title — while doing works of mercy Christ is called "a man," but "now when He comes to vengeance, the man is dropped, and He is called only a King."

The grammatical-historical commentators confirm and qualify. Ellicott reads "armies" as finding "an approximate fulfilment first in the destruction of Jerusalem, and afterwards in all times of trouble that fall upon nations and churches as the punishment of unbelief," and prefers "troops" to "armies," since the Greek strateumata denotes "bands of soldiers, not grand armies" — a point Robertson echoes. Clarke notes a textual reading, that "this very king" (ekeinos) sent his troops, supported by ancient manuscripts.

The Puritan Henry expounds the providence behind the event. "The Roman armies were his armies, of his raising, of his sending against the people of his wrath" (Isaiah 10:6) — God making use of them "to serve his own purposes, though they mean not so" (Isaiah 10:7). He identifies the crying sin that brought the ruin: not merely despising the call but "their being murderers" of his servants, "as if God were more jealous for the lives of his ministers than for the honour of his gospel." The Reformed Calvin generalises the warning: "the same destruction which Christ denounces against the Jews awaits all the ungodly who violently oppose the ministers of the Gospel," for "those who are so entirely occupied with earthly cares as to set no value on the divine invitation will at length perish miserably in famine and want." Wesley and Gill concur: the troops are "the Roman armies employed of God," destroying "primarily the Jews." Gill adds that the king "heard" of the outrage as the omniscient God whose servants' blood "calls for vengeance," so that the burning of the city is no arbitrary fury but the just answer of heaven to the murder of its messengers.
8:
"Then saith he to his servants, The wedding is ready, but they which were bidden were not worthy." The witnesses read the verse as the formal forfeiture of the Jews' privilege and the pivot toward the Gentiles. The grammatical-historical Barnes anchors the "not worthy" in the chain of Scripture that runs through the chapter, cross-referencing the apostolic verdict at Antioch — "ye put it from you, and judge yourselves unworthy of everlasting life" (Acts 13:46) — and the promise that the worthy shall walk in white (Revelation 3:4; 22:14). The worthiness in view, that is, is not a merit they lacked but a privilege they despised.

The Wesleyan Clarke presses the moral cause of the unworthiness: "because they made light of it, and would not come, preferring earthly things to heavenly blessings." He illuminates the gravity of the refusal with a custom from the wider East, noting that "among the Mohammedans, refusal to come to a marriage feast, when invited, is considered a breach of the law of God," so that "any one who shall be invited to a dinner, and does not accept the invitation, disobeys God"; the parable's hearers would have felt the insult keenly. And he turns it to gospel use: a man "may be said to be worthy of, or fit for, this marriage feast, when, feeling his wretchedness and misery, he comes to God in the way appointed, to get an entrance into the holiest by the blood of Jesus." Wesley adds only that the highways are properly "the by-ways, or turnings of the road."

The Puritan Henry (across these verses) frames the same truth in his recurring refrain: "it is not owing to God that sinners perish, but to themselves." He likens the Jews to Israel within sight of Canaan, where "the land of promise was ready, the milk and honey ready, but their unbelief and murmuring shut them out" (1 Corinthians 10:11; Hebrews 3:16). The wedding stands prepared; the unworthiness is wholly of their own contracting, and the feast will not therefore stand empty, for the king's purpose to honour his son will be fulfilled with other guests. Gill notes simply that the servants now addressed are those "preserved from their rage, who outlived their implacable enemies, and saw their utter ruin."
9:
"Go ye therefore into the highways, and as many as ye shall find, bid to the marriage." The witnesses are united that this is the calling of the Gentiles, and they fill out the picture from custom and prophecy. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the place. Barnes reads "the highways" as the squares and principal streets, "a place of confluence where persons of all descriptions would be seen," representing the offer of the gospel to the Gentiles, "commonly regarded among the Jews as living in highways and hedges — cast out, poor, and despised." Robertson glosses the Greek diexodous tōn hodōn as the "partings of the highways," the cross-streets and by-ways leading out of the city. Lightfoot supplies the social background, citing the rabbinic record that "it was a custom among rich men to invite poor travellers to feasts."

The Wesleyan Clarke and Ellicott both find in the verse "the final rejection of the Jews and the calling of the Gentiles." Clarke describes the invited as "miserable vagabonds, or the most indigent poor," wandering the country or sitting "by the sides of the ways and hedges, imploring relief," and draws the universal note: "God sends his salvation to every soul, that all may believe and be saved." Ellicott illustrates it from Paul's turning at Pisidian Antioch from the Jews who counted themselves "unworthy of eternal life" to the Gentiles who were willing to receive it (Acts 13:46).

The Reformed Calvin gives the calling its prophetic and admonitory edge. The Gentiles are summoned "from the streets and from the highways, as strangers and unknown persons," to occupy the place the friends and domestics had treated with indifference — fulfilling the song of Moses, "I will provoke them to jealousy by those which are not a people" (Deuteronomy 32:21), and the ingrafting of the wild branches (Romans 11:17). Yet he turns the warning back upon the Church: "if at that time the natural branches were not spared, the same punishment will this day be inflicted on us, if we do not answer to his call. The supper which had been prepared for us will not be lost, but God will invite other guests." Gill adds that the highways denote Gentile sinners, "far off, walking in their own ways, and in the high road to destruction."
10:
"So those servants went out into the highways, and gathered together all as many as they found, both bad and good: and the wedding was furnished with guests." The witnesses agree that the mixed gathering is the visible Church, and most cross-reference the kindred parable of the dragnet. The Wesleyan Clarke gives the fullest catalogue, reading the assembly as "the visible Church of Christ," and gathering the Lord's own images for its mingled state: "the Floor where the wheat and the chaff are mingled (Matthew 3:12); the Field where the bastard wheat and the true grain grow together (Matthew 13:26); the Net which collects of all kinds, both good and bad (Matthew 13:48); the House in which the wise and foolish are found (Matthew 25:1); and the Fold in which there are both sheep and goats (Matthew 25:33)."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott draws the same two implications as the dragnet (Matthew 13:47-48): first, "the universality of the offer of the gospel, so that none were shut out through any previous sins"; second, that "the assembly so gathered answers to the visible Church in which the evil are mingled with the good, waiting for the coming of the King to see the guests." He and Robertson note a textual point of some interest: the best manuscripts read not "the wedding was furnished" but "the bride-chamber (nymphōn) was filled," the word that in Matthew 9:15 means the bridechamber. Barnes presses the doctrinal balance: "all descriptions of people; none are good by nature — if they were they would not need the gospel — but some are worse than others, and they have special need of it."

The Reformed Gill sees in the servants' going out the turning of the apostles "from the Jews to the Gentiles, particularly the Apostle Paul, with Barnabas." The Puritan Henry (across these verses) had already drawn out the "mixed multitude, both bad and good — some that before their conversion were sober and well-inclined, as the devout Greeks (Acts 17:4) and Cornelius; others that had run to an excess of riot, as the Corinthians (1 Corinthians 6:11)." Wesley sums the manner of the gathering in three words: "by preaching every where." The verse thus prepares for the scrutiny to come: the net encloses good fish and bad, "but the Lord knows them that are his."
11:
"And when the king came in to see the guests, he saw there a man which had not on a wedding garment." With this verse the parable turns from the rejection of the Jews to the scrutiny of professing Christians, and the witnesses converge on the meaning of the garment while debating its precise content. Several note the Oriental custom that lies behind the image. Barnes and Clarke explain that kings provided festal robes for their guests — "long white robes" — so that to appear in common dress was "an expression of the highest disrespect"; Clarke cites Homer's Telemachus clothed at Menelaus's feast and the caftans of Asiatic nobles, "to refuse to accept or wear which would be deemed the highest insult." Ellicott adds the royal wardrobes "filled with many thousand garments" (compare Jehu's at the temple of Baal, 2 Kings 10:22), so that the man's act "was one of wilful insult," coming "in the filthy rags of his old life" (Isaiah 64:6) instead of the "white linen" freely offered.

On the question, "what is the wedding garment?", the witnesses divide. The grammatical-historical Ellicott surveys the answers — baptism, imputed righteousness — and judges them "at once too narrow and too technical," preferring, from the analogy of Scriptural symbolism (Revelation 19:8; Isaiah 1:18), "the holiness without which no man shall see the Lord" (Hebrews 12:14), which Christ is "ever ready to impart." Barnes likewise reads it as "a garment of salvation wrought by the Saviour and dyed in his blood," refused by the hypocrite who keeps "the filthy rags of his own righteousness."

The Reformed Calvin pronounces the dispute "whether it be faith or a holy life" a "useless controversy, for faith cannot be separated from good works, nor do good works proceed from any other source than from faith." He insists the garment is freely supplied — "whomsoever the Lord invites he at the same time supplies with clothing" (Ezekiel 16; Romans 13:14) — so the man is condemned not for failing to procure a costly robe but for being "found in his pollution." The Puritan Henry reads it as "a frame of heart and a course of life agreeable to the gospel" (Ephesians 4:1), the righteousness of saints and Christ made righteousness to them (Revelation 19:8). The Wesleyan Wesley names it "the righteousness of Christ, first imputed, then implanted." Cyril of Jerusalem turns it to the catechumens: "begin at once to wash your robes in repentance, that when called to the bride-chamber ye may be found clean."
12:
"Friend, how camest thou in hither, not having a wedding garment? And he was speechless." The witnesses dwell on two words — the address "friend" and the silence — and on the justice they reveal. On "friend," the grammatical-historical commentators and Clarke insist the Greek hetaire means "companion," not a term of affection: "he may well be termed a companion," writes Clarke, "as being a member of the visible Church, present at all those ordinances," but not truly a friend. Barnes agrees the word "does not imply friendship," and Ellicott notes it marks the act as "strange, unlooked-for, inexcusable."

On the silence, the witnesses see the self-conviction of the hypocrite. Robertson and others gloss "speechless" as the Greek ephimōthē, "muzzled, gagged," the same verb used of the ox (1 Timothy 5:18) and of the silenced Sadducees in this very chapter (v.34): "dumb from confusion and embarrassment." The man "stood mute upon his arraignment, being convicted and condemned by his own conscience."

The deepest reflection comes from the Wesleyan Clarke, who reads the man as "the emblem of those who shall perish in the last day" and draws from his case a refutation of "absolute reprobation": "Where does the doctrine of preterition appear in his case? Christ died, the sacrifice was offered, for him; the ministers of the Gospel invited him; the Holy Spirit strove with him; he might have been saved, but he was not: and the fault lies so absolutely at his own door" that God is vindicated, sending him to perdition "not for the lack of what he could not get, but for the lack of what he might have had." The Puritan Henry presses the same self-examination: "It is good for those that have a place in the church often to put it to themselves, How came I in hither? Have I a wedding garment?" The Wesleyan Wesley fixes the garment as "the righteousness of Christ, first imputed, then implanted," and warns that the passage "has no relation to the Lord's Supper, but to God's proceeding at the last day." Cyril of Jerusalem prays that none enrolled may hear "Friend, how camest thou in hither," but all may hear "Well done, good and faithful servant" (Matthew 25:21).
13:
"Bind him hand and foot, and take him away, and cast him into outer darkness; there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth." The witnesses read the sentence as the doom of the hypocrite, and several note that the parable here slides out of its own frame into the reality it figures. The Puritan Henry observes that "our Saviour here insensibly slides out of this parable into that which it intimates — the damnation of hypocrites in the other world." He reads the binding as the helplessness of the condemned, who "can neither resist nor outrun their punishment," and connects the casting out to the angels who "bind up these tares in bundles for the fire" (Matthew 13:41); the outer darkness is "darkness out of heaven, the land of light," and the weeping and gnashing express "great sorrow and anguish" and "the greatest rage and indignation."

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the textual and verbal detail. Ellicott notes that "take him away" is "wanting in many of the best MSS.," perhaps inserted to ease the difficulty of the man being thrust out after being bound, and draws the vivid contrast "between the bridal-chamber, with its lights and mirth and music, and the midnight darkness outside the palace." Robertson glosses "was speechless" once more as "muzzled," and "the outer darkness" as "all the blacker from the standpoint of the brilliantly lighted banquet hall." Barnes simply refers the phrase, as elsewhere in Matthew (8:12), to "the future punishment of the hypocrite."

The Reformed Gill identifies the servants who bind as "either the ministers of the Gospel, who cast out all such as appear to be without the grace of God and righteousness of Christ; or rather the angels, who will bind up the tares in bundles and burn them," gathering out of Christ's kingdom "all that offend and do iniquity." Across the schools the sense is one: the King's hospitality is wide, gathering bad and good alike, but His scrutiny is exact, and the profession that is not matched by the wedding garment of holiness ends not in the feast but in the darkness without.
14:
"For many are called, but few are chosen." The witnesses receive this as the moral of the whole parable, and they take some care to define its scope. The grammatical-historical Barnes insists the saying is "the inference from the whole parable, and not of the part about the man without the wedding garment"; it does not mean that the great mass in the Church are hypocrites, but that "the great mass in the human family, in the time of Christ, who had been called, had rejected the mercy of God." Robertson notes the verbal play between klētoi (the called) and eklektoi (the chosen), "called out from the called," and observes that Christ "evidently repeated many of his sayings many times."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott takes the "calling" to answer to the "bidding" of the parable, and the "chosen" to be "those who both accept the invitation and comply with its condition" — clothed in the wedding garment of holiness. He is careful to add that "the further truth of an election according to the foreknowledge of God the Father (1 Peter 1:2) is not here within view, but follows necessarily," and warns against fixing "time limits for the thoughts of God." The Reformed Calvin likewise restrains speculation: "I enter no farther at present into the question about the eternal election of God; for the words mean nothing more than that the external profession of faith is not a sufficient proof that God will acknowledge as his people all who appear to have accepted his invitation"; let every man examine himself, "that at the final review he may be pronounced one of the lawful guests" (2 Timothy 2:19).

The Alexandrian Origen reads the few chosen through his doctrine of the gates of Hades, joining the saying to "strive to enter in by the narrow door" (Luke 13:24) and "narrow is the gate that leadeth unto life, and few there be that find it" (Matthew 7:14). The Wesleyan voices keep it practical: Clarke takes it from "the Roman custom of raising their militia, where all were mustered but only those chosen who were found proper," and bids the reader "make sure work for eternity"; Wesley distils, "Many hear; few believe. Yea, many are members of the visible, but few of the invisible Church." The Puritan Henry, summing the parable, finds the chosen few once "all the careless, all the hypocritical, all the profane" are set aside — "many called to the wedding feast, but few chosen to the wedding garment, that is, to salvation, by sanctification of the Spirit."
15:
"Then went the Pharisees, and took counsel how they might entangle him in his talk." The witnesses read this as the opening of a coordinated campaign of ensnaring questions. The Antiochene Pseudo-Chrysostom gives the memorable image: "as when one seeks to dam a stream of running water, as soon as one outlet is stopped up it makes another channel for itself; so the malevolence of the Jews, foiled on one hand, seeks itself out another course." Chrysostom notes that they sent "their disciples and Herod's soldiers together, that whatever opinion He might give might be found fault with," themselves afraid "to lay hands on Him because of the populace."

The Latin Jerome supplies the political background that makes the trap intelligible: Judaea had lately been made tributary under Augustus, and "there was a great division among the people," some holding tribute owed to Rome for the security its arms maintained, the Pharisees contending that "the people of God ought not to be subject to human laws." The Herodians, by contrast, "paid tribute to the Romans, and were not devoted to the worship of God."

The Puritan Henry expounds the design most fully. The Pharisees, foiled in their challenge to Christ's authority, now "will try whether they can deal with him by their learning in the law"; it was foretold that "the rulers would take counsel against him" (Psalm 2:2), as they had against Jeremiah (Jeremiah 18:18). Their aim was "to make a man an offender for a word" (Isaiah 29:21), to bring him "into such a dilemma that he must make himself liable to the displeasure either of the Jewish multitude or of the Roman magistrates." The Reformed Calvin and the grammatical-historical Robertson concur that the alliance of Pharisees and Herodians — natural enemies — against Christ shows how "those that oppose one another continue in an opposition to Christ and his kingdom" (Henry), Samson's foxes looking several ways yet tied at the tails to one firebrand. Gill notes they had stayed to hear the foregoing parables, "saw themselves designed," and retired to take counsel rather than risk the people.
16:
"Master, we know that thou art true, and teachest the way of God in truth, neither carest thou for any man." The witnesses dwell on the treacherous flattery of the preface and on the truth it unwittingly spoke. The Latin Gloss analyses it as a triple guard against the three ways one might fail to teach truth: "We know that thou art true" (the teacher must know and love it), "teachest the way of God in truth" (he must not, from fear of God, suppress it), and "carest for no man" (he must not, from fear or favour of men, withhold it). Jerome calls it "a smooth and treacherous enquiry," meant to provoke an answer that would "fear God rather than Caesar."

The Puritan Henry gives the verse its fullest treatment, separating the truth of the words from the malice of the intent. What they said of Christ "was right, and, whether they knew it or no, blessed be God, we know it": that He is "a faithful Teacher" — "the Amen, the faithful Witness," teaching "the way of God in truth" — and "a bold Reprover" who "regarded not the person of man." Yet "there was nothing but flattery and treachery in the intention," for "they called him Master when they were contriving to treat him as the worst of malefactors"; it was "hatred covered with deceit, and a wicked heart with burning lips" (Proverbs 26:23), like Judas who kissed and betrayed. "It is the grossest atheism," Henry adds, "to think to put a cheat upon Christ, who searches the heart" (Revelation 2:23).

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators add the historical identification. Jerome and Henry explain the Herodians as those devoted to Herod and to paying Rome's tribute; Ellicott (with JFB) takes them as "the Galilean Sadducees," the tetrarch's party, allied with the Pharisees against Christ. Robertson and Lightfoot note the studied courtesy of men who hoped that "deceived by this show of honour, He might in simplicity open all His heart to them." Calvin observes that they approached with feigned reverence precisely because they "dared not openly assail" him. The flattery, all agree, was the bait on a hook already baited with a question designed to destroy.
17:
"Tell us therefore, What thinkest thou? Is it lawful to give tribute unto Caesar, or not?" The witnesses expound the cunning of the dilemma and the live political grievance behind it. The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices set out the trap plainly: if Jesus said the tribute was lawful, the Pharisees would "incense the people against him"; if unlawful, the Herodians would "incense the government against him" (Henry). Either answer ran him, in Henry's phrase, "into a premunire."

The Puritan Henry expounds the underlying question — "Has Caesar a right to demand it?" — against the history of Israel's subjugation. The nation had been "conquered by the Roman sword, and made subject to the Roman yoke"; by this very tribute "it appeared that the sceptre was departed from Judah" (Genesis 49:10), so that, "if they had understood the signs of the times, they must have concluded that Shiloh was come." The ground of their scruple was that they were Abraham's seed and "should not by consent be in bondage to any man" (John 8:33), and that God had forbidden them to "set a stranger over them." Henry calls this "an old mistake, arising from that pride and haughty spirit which bring destruction and a fall" — the same obstinacy that had refused Jeremiah's counsel to submit to Babylon (Jeremiah 27:12) and that "in a few years brought final destruction upon them by the Romans."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott and the Wesleyan Wesley note how genuinely the question divided opinion, "a case which relates to practice, and occurs daily," so that the demand "What thinkest thou?" wore the look of an honest appeal for a ruling. Gill observes that they hoped, whichever way he answered, "to expose him to the fury either of the jealous Jews or of the jealous Romans," and were ready to triumph "as Pharaoh did over Israel, that the wilderness had shut him in." The whole art of the snare lay in making a real and painful question — the tension between covenant privilege and imperial subjection — serve as a trap for the One who alone could resolve it.
18:
"But Jesus perceived their wickedness, and said, Why tempt ye me, ye hypocrites?" The witnesses fasten on the Lord's discernment of the heart behind the smooth words. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note that He answered not their speech but their intent — Ellicott observing that "He read their thoughts," and that the very word "hypocrites" exposes the mask of reverence they had assumed. Where they had called him "Master" while plotting his ruin, He returns no compliment but a charge, naming the wickedness they had hidden.

The Antiochene Pseudo-Chrysostom (commenting across these verses) draws the principle that "God answers men's hearts, and not their words": Christ "calls them hypocrites, that, seeing Him to be a discerner of human hearts, they might not be hardy enough to carry through their design"; and he adds the paradox that "God's wrath is more profitable to man than man's favour," for the Pharisees "spoke fair that they might destroy Him, but Jesus put them to shame that He might save them."

The Reformed Calvin reads the rebuke as the unmasking of a settled malice: the question was "not put with honest intentions, nor from a desire of instruction, but by cunning and deceit," and Christ, "regarding their plot," lays it bare before they can spring it. The Wesleyan Wesley notes the same penetrating insight by which the Lord turns every snare into an occasion of instruction. Across the schools the verse is read as a demonstration of Christ's deity and wisdom: the heart-searcher cannot be flattered or trapped, and the tempters, thinking to catch him in his talk, are themselves caught in their own. The naming of them as "hypocrites" is at once judgment and mercy — judgment on the deceit, and a summons to the shame that might yet have led them to repentance. Gill adds that Christ perceived their wickedness "without hearing any of their debates, but by his omniscience," knowing "the malice and design of their hearts"; so the verse stands among the chapter's proofs of his deity, for the One they sought to ensnare reads every secret thought, and turns the cunning of the tempter into the occasion of his own exposure.
19:
"Shew me the tribute money. And they brought unto him a penny." The witnesses use the request to show both Christ's wisdom and a small confirmation of his true humanity. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the coin: the Greek dēnarion, a Roman denarius, the standard tribute-coin, bearing (as Jerome notes) the image not of Augustus but "Tiberius, under whom also the Lord suffered." Lightfoot and the expositors observe that the tax was paid in Roman coin stamped with the emperor's head, the very token of subjection that the Pharisees resented.

The Latin Jerome draws a doctrinal caution from the asking itself: "Let those who think that the Saviour asks because He is ignorant learn from the present place that it is not so; for at all events Jesus must have known whose image was on the coin." The question is not the question of ignorance but of a teacher leading his hearers, by their own answer, to the conclusion he intends — "the tempters are best confuted out of their own words." By calling for the coin, Christ makes them produce the evidence that condemns their pretended scruple: they themselves carry and use the emperor's currency.

The Reformed Calvin and Gill see in the manoeuvre the perfect wisdom that "does ever wisely" (Jerome): rather than pronounce abstractly, Christ fixes the argument upon a visible fact in their own hands. Robertson and the grammatical voices note that the production of the denarius silently settled half the case before a word of judgment was spoken — for to handle and carry the imperial coinage was already to acknowledge, in daily practice, the authority they affected to dispute. The small physical demand, that is, turns the trap inside out: the men who came to catch Christ in his talk are first made to answer with their own purses, and the coin they hand him becomes the text of the saying that follows. The witnesses note, too, the quiet self-betrayal in the act: men who scrupled over the lawfulness of the tribute carried the tribute-coin ready in their purses, so that the simple call for the denarius had, before a further word, half-decided the case against them out of their own practice.
20:
"And he saith unto them, Whose is this image and superscription?" The witnesses treat the brief question as the hinge of the whole exchange, turning the Pharisees' trap into their own confession. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the coin bore the emperor's head and titular inscription — his "image and superscription" — and that Christ's question compels the questioners to name Caesar themselves, so that the verdict will come, in effect, from their own mouths.

Lightfoot and the expositors observe the rabbinic principle that ran in the background: that the prevailing coinage of a realm was itself a token of sovereignty, the accepted currency proclaiming whose dominion the people lived under. By asking whose image the coin bore, Christ silently invokes that very principle. The Jews who used Caesar's money in their daily dealings had already, by that use, acknowledged the order under which they lived; the question only draws the admission into the light.

The whole force of the verse, across the schools, lies in its economy. Christ does not argue the politics of tribute or weigh the rights of empire against the privileges of the covenant; he simply holds up the coin and asks a question a child could answer. The Pharisees, who had framed an elaborate dilemma, are reduced to a single flat word of reply, and that reply furnishes the premise for the saying to follow. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott and Robertson all read the question as the pivot on which the Lord turns aside the snare: by making the tempters acknowledge whose image they carry, he prepares the ground for the great distinction between what is owed to Caesar and what is owed to God. The image stamped on the metal becomes, in the next verse, the figure for the image stamped on man; and the question "whose is this?" opens, beneath the surface of a tax dispute, the deeper question of whose we ourselves are. Across the witnesses the lesson is that Christ's method is to make the truth visible: he does not lecture on sovereignty but holds up a coin, and the image stamped upon the metal carries the argument that the next verse will complete, the question 'whose is this?' opening into the question of whose we ourselves are.
21:
"Render therefore unto Caesar the things which are Caesar's; and unto God the things that are God's." The witnesses receive this as one of the Lord's most weighty sayings, and they expound it along complementary lines without real conflict. The Latin fathers read it through the image on the coin. Hilary draws the parallel that became classic: "Caesar's coin is in the gold, in which his image is portrayed; God's coin is that on which the divine image is stamped" — so "give your money to Caesar, but preserve a conscience void of offence for God"; we owe to God "body, soul, and will." Jerome notes that the Lord himself "rendered to Caesar tribute, both for Himself and for Peter," and rendered to God the things of God "in doing the will of His Father."

The Antiochene Chrysostom adds the crucial qualification: when you hear the command to render to Caesar, "know that such things only are intended which in nothing are opposed to religion; if such there be, it is no longer Caesar's but the Devil's tribute." Lest any think Christ subjected them merely to man, He balanced the saying with "and unto God the things that are God's." The Alexandrian Origen reads it morally: "we ought to give some things to the body as a tribute to Caesar, that is, necessaries," and "such things as are congenial to our souls' nature... we ought to offer to God" — censuring alike the Pharisees who "forbid to give tribute to Caesar" by over-rigour (1 Timothy 4:3) and the Herodians who indulge the flesh; or, more sternly, "the prince of this world is called Caesar," and we render him his due by "casting off all wickedness."

The grammatical-historical, Reformed and Wesleyan voices keep to the plain political-and-religious balance: civil dues are owed to the civil power, and supreme allegiance to God, the two not in conflict but in their proper orders. Jerome records the upshot: "they marvelled, and left Him, and went their way," carrying off "their unbelief and wonder together." Cyril of Jerusalem cites the saying among the texts confessing Christ's authority. So the verse, which the tempters meant to divide Christ from either Rome or the people, instead lays down the abiding rule that renders each its own — Caesar his coin, and God the whole self that bears His image.
22:
"When they had heard these words, they marvelled, and left him, and went their way." The witnesses read the close of the tribute episode as a quiet triumph of wisdom over malice. The Latin Jerome notes that "they who ought to have believed did but wonder at His great wisdom, that their craft had found no means for ensnaring Him," and that they departed "carrying away their unbelief and wonder together" — astonishment that fell short of faith.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators dwell on this very combination of amazement and impenitence. To marvel at Christ and yet to leave him is, in their reading, the tragedy of the merely convinced: the tempters acknowledge his wisdom by their wonder and reject it by their going. Gill observes that, baffled and confounded, "they left him," their elaborate snare broken by a single saying. Calvin notes that the same penetrating answer which silenced them ought to have subdued them, but that obstinate men, "whenever they are reduced to extremities, to avoid being compelled to yield to the truth, are accustomed to introduce something which is foreign to the subject" — or, failing that, to withdraw.

The witnesses thus make the verse a study in the limits of mere conviction. Christ had not only escaped the trap but had laid down a principle that wronged neither Caesar nor God; the questioners could find no flaw, and yet the wonder it produced led not to discipleship but to retreat. Barnes, Clarke and Ellicott all note that this withdrawal is not the end of the day's assaults — the Sadducees come next, and after them the lawyer — so that the marvelling departure of the Pharisees and Herodians is but one wave in a rising tide of hostility, each party in turn confounded and each in turn unconverted. The verse stands, then, as a warning written into the narrative itself: a man may be silenced by the truth, may even admire it, and still walk away from the One who speaks it. Gill notes that they departed "baffled and confounded," and the witnesses agree that this baffled withdrawal, repeated by each party in turn through the chapter, is the recurring shape of the day: the truth prevails in argument, and yet the heart that meets it unconverted finds no remedy in being silenced.
23:
"The same day came to him the Sadducees, which say that there is no resurrection." The witnesses introduce the third assault and identify the sect with care. The Latin Jerome distinguishes the two parties: the Pharisees "pretended to the righteousness of traditions," the Sadducees (whose name he derives from "righteous") "denied the resurrection of body and soul, and confessed neither Angel nor spirit" (Acts 23:8), receiving "only the five books of Moses, rejecting the Prophets." The Alexandrian Origen adds that they "not only denied the resurrection of the body, but took away the immortality of the soul," and that the two things Christ charges them with not knowing — "the Scriptures and the power of God" — are the very grounds of the resurrection; indeed by "the power of God" Origen hears Christ pointing to Himself, "the power of God" (1 Corinthians 1:24), whom they did not know.

The Antiochene Chrysostom and Pseudo-Chrysostom set the trap in its Mosaic frame. Because death "seemed to the Jews an unmixed evil, Moses ordered that the wife of one who died without sons should be given to his brother, that a son might be born to the dead man," some "alleviation of death." Pseudo-Chrysostom diagnoses the deeper strategy: the devil, unable to crush religion outright, "brought in the sect of the Sadducees denying the resurrection, thus breaking down all purpose of a righteous life; for who would endure a daily struggle against himself, unless he looked to the hope of the resurrection?"

The Puritan Henry gives the sect its full character — "much of the genius of the Epicureans," holding "no future state, no judgment to come," that "when the body dies, the soul is annihilated" — and observes the irony that "the Pharisees and Sadducees were contrary to each other, and yet confederates against Christ," so that "Christ's gospel hath always suffered between superstitious hypocrites on the one hand and profane infidels on the other." The Reformed Gill and the grammatical-historical Lightfoot note that the Sadducees came "with a knotty question with which they had often puzzled the Pharisees," cavilling, in Lightfoot's report, "the cloud faileth and passeth away; so he that goeth down to the grave doth not return."
24:
"Master, Moses said, If a man die, having no children, his brother shall marry his wife, and raise up seed unto his brother." The witnesses identify the law and its purpose, treating the Sadducees' citation as the bait of their trap. The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators locate the statute in Deuteronomy 25:5, illustrated in the practice of Ruth 4:5, and explain it, with Henry, as "a political law, founded in the particular constitution of the Jewish commonwealth, to preserve the distinction of families and inheritances."

The Antiochene Chrysostom had already noted (on the preceding verse) the deeper rationale: the levirate marriage was meant to be "some alleviation of death" for a people to whom death "seemed an unmixed evil," securing that the dead man's "name should not perish" and his house be established by the nearest kin, "whose kindred obliged him thereto." The Sadducees, by invoking Moses, sought to confute the resurrection out of the very Scriptures they accepted, building their fable on a law of marriage to overthrow the doctrine of the life to come.

The Puritan Henry draws the moral and devotional notes the case affords, observing that the brothers obeyed the law "though they had a power of refusal under the penalty of a reproach" (Deuteronomy 25:7), and that "discouraging providences should not keep us from doing our duty, because we must be governed by the rule, not by the event." He treats the supposed case as one that, "whether a case in fact or only a moot case, is not at all material; if it had not really occurred, yet possibly it might." The Wesleyan Clarke and Wesley keep to the plain identification of the Mosaic ordinance, and Gill notes that the Sadducees had "often puzzled the Pharisees" with just such a riddle, hoping now to "nonplus Christ" by it. Across the schools the verse is read as the careful laying of a snare: a genuine law of Israel, soberly stated, made the platform for an attempt to prove the resurrection absurd. Robertson and the expositors note that the Sadducees cite the statute exactly, with no quarrel of their own against Moses, precisely so that the absurdity they mean to draw out shall seem to follow from the Law itself rather than from their unbelief; the sober accuracy of the citation is part of the craft of the snare.
25:
"Now there were with us seven brethren: and the first, when he had married a wife, deceased, and, having no issue, left his wife unto his brother." The witnesses treat the constructed case briefly, reading it as the Sadducees' attempt to make the resurrection collapse into absurdity. The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices note that the example, whether real or hypothetical, was framed to press their objection to its sharpest: seven successive husbands of one woman, all childless, so that no single claim could be preferred in a supposed risen life.

The Puritan Henry draws from the case the providential reflections it invites. It supposes "the desolations that death sometimes makes in families when it comes with commission, how it often sweeps away a whole fraternity in a little time" — "when there were seven brothers grown up to man's estate, there was a family very likely to be built up, and yet this numerous family leaves neither son nor nephew" (Job 18:19). "Well may we say then, Except the Lord build the house, they labour in vain that build it." He commends, too, the obedience of the brothers in marrying under the law, judging that the seventh, who "ventured last," if he did it "purely in obedience to God, was a good man, and one that made conscience of his duty."

Gill, Barnes, Clarke and Wesley keep to the plain sense, taking the seven brethren as the successive parties to the supposed marriage, and noting that the Sadducees pile up the instances precisely to make the question of "whose wife" she should be insoluble. The mounting tally of husbands is the Sadducees' rhetorical art: each death, far from raising any difficulty for them, is meant to multiply the embarrassment they will press in the question to come, turning a sober law of Israel into a reductio against the hope of the world to come. Gill, Barnes and Wesley keep to the plain reading that the first brother's childless death set the chain in motion, each successive marriage being demanded by the same law, so that the woman passed in turn from husband to husband as the statute required and the Sadducees' fiction needed.
26:
"Likewise the second also, and the third, unto the seventh." The verse draws the briefest comment, both witnesses simply registering the repetition that completes the Sadducees' hypothetical. Gill and Barnes note that the successive marriages of the second brother and the rest, down to the seventh, carry forward the single device of the objection: one woman, seven husbands, no issue from any, so that the supposed difficulty for the resurrection is heaped up to its full height.

The witnesses read the mounting list as deliberate rhetorical construction. The Sadducees do not need seven brothers to state their cavil — two would have served — but the larger number sharpens the apparent absurdity they are building toward, multiplying the claimants until the question "whose wife shall she be?" seems to admit no answer. Each repetition, soberly worded as a continuation of the Mosaic case, is in truth another stone laid on the structure of their unbelief.

Read in the context the other witnesses supply across this passage, the verse belongs to a snare that assumes, as Henry observed, that a risen life must be "a return to such a state as this we are now in," with the same marriages and the same circumstances. That assumption is the hidden flaw the Lord will expose, for the whole edifice of the seven marriages rests upon it. The very tidiness of the construction — first, second, third, unto the seventh — exhibits the Sadducees' confidence that they have devised an unanswerable case; and it is precisely that confidence, grounded in ignorance both of the Scriptures and of the power of God, which Christ will overturn. The reticence of the commentators here matches the function of the verse: it is a step in an argument, not a resting-place, and its whole weight is thrown forward upon the question and the answer still to come. So the witnesses, sparing of comment, let the bare tally stand for what it is: a carefully built ladder of marriages whose every rung is laid only to make the question of the next verse seem unanswerable, and whose whole weight rests on the unexamined assumption that the life to come must mirror the life that now is.
27:
"And last of all the woman died also." The witnesses note in this short verse the completion of the Sadducees' case and, in passing, a sober reflection on mortality. Gill and Barnes register that the death of the woman closes the hypothetical: all eight parties — the seven brothers and the wife they successively married — have now passed through death, so that the stage is fully set for the question the Sadducees mean to spring.

The Puritan Henry (across these verses) draws the lesson that lies beneath the bare statement: "survivorship is but a reprieve; they that live long, and bury their relations and neighbours one after another, do not thereby acquire an immortality." The woman who had outlived seven husbands dies at last like the rest, for "Death's bitter cup goes round, and, sooner or later, we must all pledge in it" (Jeremiah 25:26). The very structure of the Sadducees' fable, by which death claims one after another until none is left, becomes in Henry's hands a meditation on the universality of the grave — the more pointed because the Sadducees told the tale to deny that the grave is anything but the end.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott and the other expositors keep to the narrative function: with the woman's death the case is complete, and every claim upon her now stands, in the Sadducees' reckoning, on equal footing, so that the question "whose wife shall she be?" can be put with full force. The irony the witnesses feel, without always stating it, is that the very tale designed to mock the resurrection rehearses death's total dominion — eight deaths in a single family — and so silently underscores the need of precisely that hope the Sadducees deny. The verse, then, is both the last brick in their argument and an unwitting witness to the reign of death that only the resurrection answers. Gill and Barnes note that with this the Sadducees' construction is complete and symmetrical — seven husbands and one wife, all dead, none surviving to press a rival claim — so that the question may be put, as they suppose, without any escape; the very neatness of the fiction is the measure of their confidence and, in the event, of their fall.
28:
"Therefore in the resurrection whose wife shall she be of the seven? for they all had her." Here the Sadducees spring the trap they have built, and the witnesses expose the false assumption on which it rests. The Reformed Gill and the grammatical-historical commentators note that the whole force of the question depends on supposing that the risen life must reproduce the marriages and conditions of this one — that the woman must in the resurrection still be wife to one of the seven.

The Puritan Henry diagnoses the error precisely: "they take it for granted that, if there be a resurrection, it must be a return to such a state as this we are now in, and to the same circumstances," like "the imaginary Platonic year"; and on that supposition the case is "an invincible absurdity." But the supposition is the flaw. Henry adds a shrewd observation about the source of the Sadducees' error: "the Pharisees, who professed to believe a resurrection, had very gross and carnal notions concerning it," expecting "the delights and pleasures of the animal life," and "nothing gives greater advantage to atheism and infidelity than the carnality of those that make religion a servant to their sensual appetites" — "while those that are erroneous deny the truth, those that are superstitious betray it to them."

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices keep to the structure of the snare: the seven equal claims are meant to make the resurrection ridiculous, since no ground appears for preferring one husband over another. JFB and the expositors observe that the Sadducees thought they had at last devised "a most convincing argument in favour of their error." Yet, as the witnesses across this passage agree, the argument turns wholly on a carnal picture of the world to come; once that picture is corrected — as Christ is about to correct it — the difficulty dissolves of itself. The question "whose wife shall she be?" has no answer only because it asks the wrong thing, projecting the marriages of earth into a state where, as the next verses will show, they have no place.
29:
"Jesus answered and said unto them, Ye do err, not knowing the scriptures, nor the power of God." The witnesses make this verse the heart of the Lord's reply, the double diagnosis of the Sadducees' error. The Puritan Henry expounds it most fully. "Ignorance is the cause of error; those that are in the dark miss their way." The two things they do not know answer to the two halves of their unbelief. First, "they know not the power of God, which would lead men to infer that there may be a resurrection"; for when we are told "that a dead body, turned into common and undistinguished dust, shall be raised the same body, we are ready to say, How can these things be?" — yet "if we firmly believe in God the Father Almighty, that nothing is impossible with God, all these difficulties vanish" (Acts 26:8). Second, "they know not the scriptures, which decidedly affirm that there shall be a resurrection and a future state" — the scope "both of the law and of the prophets" (Acts 24:14), foreseen by Job (Job 19:26), Ezekiel (Ezekiel 37), and Daniel (Daniel 12:2).

The Reformed Calvin and the grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott concur in the diagnosis: error springs from ignorance, and ignorance here is twofold — of the written word that promises the resurrection, and of the divine power that effects it. Henry notes the gentleness of the reproof: Christ rebukes "with the meekness of wisdom, and is not so sharp upon them as sometimes he was upon the chief priests," yet the verdict is plain — "those do greatly err, in the judgment of Christ, who deny the resurrection and a future state."

The Alexandrian Origen (across this passage) had already joined the two: by "the power of God" he hears Christ pointing to Himself, the power by which "the resurrection and the new life in it" is brought to pass, whom they knew not "as not knowing the Scriptures which spoke of Him." The Wesleyan Wesley keeps the plain edge that the Sadducees' whole system rested on a double ignorance, curable only by submission to the Scriptures and to the God who raises the dead.
30:
"For in the resurrection they neither marry, nor are given in marriage, but are as the angels of God in heaven." The witnesses read this as the correction of the Sadducees' false premise: the risen life is not a continuation of the present order but a transformed and angelic state. The Puritan Henry expounds the change. In the present state marriage is necessary, "instituted in innocency," for the gratifying of natural desire and the recruiting of "the deficiencies of human nature"; but in the resurrection "there is no occasion for marriage," for "where God will be all in all, there needs no other meet-help; the body will be spiritual, and there will be in it no carnal desires to be gratified," nor any decay of individuals requiring renewal of the race (Malachi 2:15).

The Antiochene Pseudo-Chrysostom (on the preceding verse) had given the rationale Christ here implies: "we marry to the end that what death consumes, birth may replenish; therefore where the law of death is taken away, the cause of birth is taken away likewise." And he notes the fitness of the angelic comparison precisely here: Christ brings in the angels not when speaking of fasting or alms but only "where He speaks of the ceasing of marriage," because chastity above all the virtues "is an angelic act," by which "our nature is bound to the other virtues."

The Latin Hilary and the grammatical-historical Ellicott take "as the angels" to assure us that "our conversation in heaven shall be spiritual"; Jerome notes the saying is spoken of beings "having capacity for marriage" who shall yet not marry, not of insensate things. The Reformed Calvin and the Wesleyan Wesley keep the plain doctrine: the resurrection state transcends the conditions of earthly life, the relations of this world giving place to the immediate vision and service of God. The verse thus dismantles the Sadducees' case at its foundation — there is no "whose wife" in the resurrection, because the very institution on which their riddle depended belongs only to the world that passes away. Gill adds that in the resurrection the saints "shall be as the angels," not in nature but in this, that they neither marry nor die; and so the witnesses agree that the whole knot of the Sadducees' riddle is untied at a stroke, the supposed difficulty vanishing with the earthly institution on which alone it depended.
31:
"But as touching the resurrection of the dead, have ye not read that which was spoken unto you by God?" The witnesses note that Christ now turns from correcting the Sadducees' picture of the risen life to proving the doctrine itself, and that He proves it from the very Scriptures they accepted. The grammatical-historical commentators observe the pointed "have ye not read" — a reproach to men who prided themselves on the Law, yet had missed the resurrection implied within it. The proof is drawn not from the prophets, whom the Sadducees rejected, but from Moses, whom they revered.

The witnesses, here and across the passage, stress the wisdom of this choice of ground. As Jerome had observed, there were plainer texts of the resurrection — "the dead shall be raised" (Isaiah 26:19), "many of them that sleep in the dust shall awake" (Daniel 12:2) — but "it would have been foolish to bring forward testimonies whose authority they did not admit." Christ therefore meets them on their own ground, citing the words spoken to Moses at the bush, words "spoken unto you by God," so that the Sadducees cannot evade the testimony by appeal to a narrowed canon.

The phrase "spoken unto you by God" is itself weighed by the expositors: the saying is no mere human report but a divine utterance, carrying the full authority of the One who is about to be named the God of the living. Gill, Barnes, Clarke and Ellicott all read the verse as the formal introduction of the decisive proof-text, the hinge on which the whole reply turns. Cyril of Jerusalem elsewhere cites the same method — Christ confirming from the Law a truth the cavillers denied — as a model of meeting error with the Scripture it claims to honour. The verse, brief in itself, marks the transition from defence to demonstration: having dissolved the Sadducees' difficulty, the Lord now establishes the very doctrine they had set out to mock, and establishes it from the books they could not refuse. Gill, Barnes and Clarke note that the appeal 'have ye not read' fastens the charge of ignorance upon the very point of the Sadducees' pride, their mastery of Moses; the resurrection, they are shown, is written in the books they most revered, had they read them with understanding rather than with a will to deny.
32:
"I am the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob. God is not the God of the dead, but of the living." The witnesses treat this as the crux of the whole reply, and they probe both why Christ chose so seemingly indirect a text and what exactly it proves. The Latin Jerome explains the strategy: the Sadducees "received only the five books of Moses, rejecting the Prophets," so plainer testimonies "whose authority they did not admit" would have been useless; Christ therefore proves the point from Exodus, that "having established that souls abide after death — forasmuch as God could not be the God of those who had no existence — there might fitly come in the resurrection of bodies."

The Alexandrian Origen presses the precise wording: "He said not, I am the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, but the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob," each repetition marking that "each of these possessed God entirely," not "some small portion in God" as "the God of the Hebrews" might imply. The God who declares "I am that I am" (Exodus 3:14) cannot "be called the God of those who are not." The Latin Hilary reasons to the same end: the patriarchs "had gone to their rest" when this was spoken, "yet they of whom He was the God were in being; for it is not possible that which is dead should have that which is eternal."

The Puritan Henry and the Reformed and grammatical-historical voices draw the doctrinal upshot: the argument proves the soul's continued life immediately, and the resurrection of the body by consequence, since the covenant relation God owns with the patriarchs guarantees their whole persons. Augustine adds the answer to a deeper doubt — that "the earthy matter of which the flesh of men is made perishes not before God," but "into whatsoever dust reduced... is in a moment of time restored." So the text the Sadducees would have thought least relevant becomes, in Christ's hands, the unanswerable proof: the God of the patriarchs is the God of the living, and therefore Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob live to Him, and shall rise.
33:
"And when the multitude heard this, they were astonished at his doctrine." The witnesses note that the wonder belongs not to the defeated Sadducees but to the listening crowd, and they draw from it the contrast between conviction and the people's amazement. The Latin Remigius makes the point exactly: "not the Sadducees but the multitudes were astonished," and he adds the ecclesial application — "this is daily done in the Church; when by Divine inspiration the adversaries of the Church are overcome, the multitude of the faithful rejoice."

The grammatical-historical commentators observe that the astonishment was at "his doctrine" — at the unexpected power with which Christ had drawn so decisive a proof of the resurrection from a text the Sadducees themselves accepted, turning their chosen weapon against them. The crowd, who had perhaps shared something of the Pharisees' carnal notions, heard the doctrine of the world to come set on a new and surer footing, and marvelled.

Read with the witnesses' comments across this passage, the verse closes the resurrection controversy as the tribute controversy had closed — with wonder. But where the Pharisees had marvelled and withdrawn in unbelief, here the multitude is "astonished at his doctrine," and the note is one of a teaching vindicated before the people. The Latin Raban observes that Christ had answered "aptly against their unbelief," replying both to the conditions of the risen state and to the fact of the resurrection itself. The astonishment of the crowd is thus the narrative's verdict on the exchange: the Sadducees, who came confident of an unanswerable riddle, are silenced, and the doctrine they meant to ridicule stands forth so plainly established that the hearers are amazed. The verse prepares, too, for the Pharisees' regrouping in the verse that follows, drawn back into the contest precisely because they "heard that He had put the Sadducees to silence." Gill and the expositors note that the astonishment is the narrative's own verdict on the exchange: the Sadducees, confident of an unanswerable riddle, are reduced to silence, and the doctrine they meant to ridicule stands so plainly established from their own Scriptures that the listening crowd is amazed at the teaching.
34:
"But when the Pharisees had heard that he had put the Sadducees to silence, they were gathered together." The witnesses read the regrouping of the Pharisees as fresh impudence in the face of repeated defeat. The Latin Jerome marks the irony: having been "themselves already confuted in the matter of the denarius, and now seeing their adversaries also overthrown," they "should have taken warning to attempt no further deceit against Him; but hate and jealousy are the parents of impudence." So "the Pharisees and Sadducees, thus foes to one another, unite in one common purpose to tempt Jesus."

The Antiochene Pseudo-Chrysostom reads their gathering as a tactic of numbers: "the Pharisees meet together, that their numbers may silence Him whom their reasonings could not confute"; they agree that "one speak for all, and all speak through one," so that a victory might "seem to belong to all," while a defeat "may rest with Him alone." John Chrysostom finds the spring of their malice in the very commandment about to be discussed: "from having no charity, from pining with envy," they "put forward the lawyer, not desiring to learn, but making a trial of Him."

The Puritan Henry draws out the perversity of their motive: they "were gathered together, not to return him the thanks of their party for confirming the truth against the Sadducees, the common enemies of their religion, but to tempt him, in hopes to get the reputation of puzzling him who had puzzled the Sadducees." They were "more vexed that Christ was honoured, than pleased that the Sadducees were silenced," being "more concerned for their own tyranny and traditions than for the doctrine of the resurrection." It is, Henry notes, "an instance of Pharisaical envy to be displeased at the maintaining of a confessed truth, when it is done by those we do not like" — to "sacrifice a public good to private piques." The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott note that this regathering opens the last of the day's testing questions, after which "no man dared ask Him any more." Gill notes that the Pharisees, hearing the Sadducees confuted, came together not in gladness at a shared truth vindicated but in fresh design against Christ, so that the very victory which should have reconciled them to him only sharpened their resolve to renew the assault by another hand.
35:
"Then one of them, which was a lawyer, asked him a question, tempting him." The witnesses weigh the spirit of the questioner, and note an apparent difference between the Gospels. The Puritan Henry identifies the lawyer as one of the students and teachers of the Law who "dealt more in practical questions than the scribes," professing "casuistical divinity." He reads the temptation gently: "not with any design to ensnare him, as appears by St. Mark's relation, where this was he to whom Christ said, Thou art not far from the kingdom of God (Mark 12:34), but only to see what he would say, and to draw on discourse" — "rather a scornful design to catechise him as a child than a spiteful design to dispute with him as an adversary."

The Latin Augustine addresses the difficulty directly: Matthew says the man came "tempting," while Mark concludes with Christ's commendation of his wise answer. Augustine offers two solutions — that "though he came to tempt, the Lord's answer may have wrought correction within him," or that "the tempting here meant need not be that of one designing to deceive an enemy, but rather the cautious approach of one making proof of a stranger." The Antiochene Pseudo-Chrysostom takes a sterner view: "he who now enquires for the greatest commandment had not observed the least," for "he only ought to seek for a higher righteousness who has fulfilled the lower." The Alexandrian Origen makes the lawyer a type: "all who thus ask questions of any teacher to try him, and not to learn of him, we must regard as brethren of this Pharisee," calling Christ "Master" while refusing to learn.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices keep to the plain sense, noting that the question of the greatest commandment was a live dispute among the rabbis, so that the lawyer's enquiry, whether malicious or merely testing, touched a real point of debate. The witnesses thus leave a genuine debate of their own: whether this questioner was a hardened tempter or a half-sincere enquirer whom the encounter brought near the kingdom. Gill and the expositors observe that the lawyer was 'tempting him' — putting the question to test rather than to learn — yet that the very form of his enquiry, touching the heart of the Law, drew from Christ an answer that has become the church's summary of all duty, whatever the spirit in which it was first asked.
36:
"Master, which is the great commandment in the law?" The witnesses treat the question as one both needless and pointed — needless because all God's commands are great, pointed because it touched a real dispute among the rabbis. The Puritan Henry observes that it was "a needless question, when all the things of God's law are great things" (Hosea 8:12), and "the wisdom from above is without partiality" (Malachi 2:9); "yet it is true, there are some commands that are the principles of the oracles of God, more extensive and inclusive than others," as Christ himself spoke of "the weightier matters of the law" (Matthew 23:23).

Henry diagnoses the design: the question was "to try him, not so much his knowledge as his judgment," for "it was a question disputed among the critics in the law. Some would have the law of circumcision to be the great commandment, others the law of the sabbath, others the law of sacrifices, according as they severally stood affected." If Christ "should magnify one commandment, they would reflect on him as vilifying the rest." Yet Henry also notes, comparing the parallel (Luke 10:27), that "it was an adjudged point among the lawyers that the love of God and our neighbour is the great commandment," so the asking of it looked more like a scornful catechising than a genuine perplexity.

The Latin Jerome had read the malice more darkly: the lawyer "enquires not for the sake of the commands, but... that, seeing all that God commands is great, he may have occasion to cavil whatever the answer be."
