The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices keep to the plain content: the man asks for the supreme commandment, and the Lord's answer will name not one isolated precept but the principle from which all the rest flow. So the verse sets the stage for one of the great summaries of the Law, the question's hidden snare turned into an occasion for the deepest teaching on the heart of all obedience. Gill notes that the lawyer asks for 'the great commandment in the law,' and the witnesses agree that the Lord's reply will name not a single isolated precept but the love that is the spring of every other, so that the question framed to catch him becomes the occasion of the deepest and most comprehensive teaching on obedience.
37:
"Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy mind." The witnesses expound both the object of the command — love, not fear — and the meaning of its three measures. The Antiochene Pseudo-Chrysostom stresses the first: "Thou shalt love, not fear; for to love is more than to fear; to fear belongs to slaves, to love to sons." God "does not desire to be served servilely as a master, but to be loved as a father, for He has given the spirit of adoption." He then distinguishes the three: to love "with the whole heart" is to "incline the heart to the love of no one thing more than of God"; "with the whole soul" is "to have the mind stayed upon the truth, and firm in the faith"; "with the whole mind" is to have "all the faculties open and unoccupied for Him," the intellect, wisdom, thoughts, and memory all employed about God.

The Latin Augustine offers a parallel analysis: "with all thy heart, that your whole thoughts; with all thy soul, that your whole life; with all thy mind, that your whole understanding — may be given to Him from whom you have that you give." He concludes that God "has left no part of our life which may justly be unfilled of Him," so that "if aught else present itself for the soul's love, it should be absorbed into that channel in which the whole current of love runs." The Gloss compresses it: "heart, i.e. understanding; soul, i.e. will; mind, i.e. memory; so you shall think, will, remember nothing contrary to Him."

The Alexandrian Origen reads the measures as recollection, readiness to lay down life, and the bringing forth of nothing but what is of God. The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices take the threefold phrase as the demand for a love whole and undivided — sincere, strong, and supreme. So the commandment, in the witnesses' hands, requires not a faculty or an act but the entire self: the love of God is to be the one affection that gathers thought, will, life, and memory into a single Godward stream.
38:
"This is the first and great commandment." The witnesses treat this brief verse as the Lord's ranking of the love of God above all other precepts, and they probe in what sense it is "first" and "great." The Alexandrian Origen, commenting across these verses, draws the lesson from the wording: "if the Lord had given no answer, we should have judged that there was no commandment greater than the rest; but when the Lord adds, This is the first and great commandment, we learn that there is a great one, and that there are less, down to the least." And he marks the precise force: Christ "says not only that it is a great, but that it is the first commandment, not in order of Scripture, but in supremacy of value."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices follow this sense: the love of God is first not in sequence but in dignity and primacy, the spring and foundation of all the rest. As Henry expounds (across these verses), it is "the first and great commandment of all, and the summary of all the commands of the first table," for "God, being good infinitely, originally, and eternally, is to be loved in the first place, and nothing loved beside him but what is loved for him." Obedience to this command "is the spring of obedience to all the rest, which is then only acceptable when it flows from love."

The witnesses thus read the verse not as setting one duty against others but as establishing the order of all duty. Love to God is great because it is comprehensive — the affection from which every other obedience must proceed, and without which no other obedience is acceptable. Origen adds the searching qualification that only those "take upon them the greatness and supremacy of this precept" who fulfil it with the three conditions of heart, soul, and mind; the mere naming of the commandment as first does not discharge it. So the shortest verse of this exchange carries its weight: it fixes the love of God as the highest and primary of all commands, the head and source of the whole law, and the test by which every lesser obedience is measured.
39:
"And the second is like unto it, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself." The witnesses dwell on the likeness of the second commandment to the first and on the breadth of "neighbour." The Latin Augustine expounds both. He takes "neighbour" widely: "every man is to be regarded as a neighbour, because evil is to be done to no man"; even "the holy Angels" are included, who perform services for us, and even Christ himself, "for it was Himself whom He represents as the good Samaritan." He grounds the right love of self and neighbour in God: "he that loves men ought to love them either because they are righteous, or that they may be righteous; and so also ought he to love himself," and thus "without peril he may love his neighbour as himself."

The Antiochene Pseudo-Chrysostom explains why the second is "like" the first: "who loves man is as who loves God, for man is God's image, wherein God is loved, as a king is honoured in his statue." The Latin Hilary reads the likeness as identity of obligation and reward: "the second command is like the first" because "no love of God without Christ, or of Christ without God, can profit to salvation."

The Puritan Henry draws out the implied and the prescribed self-love. "There is a self-love which is corrupt, and the root of the greatest sins, and must be mortified; but there is a self-love which is natural, and the rule of the greatest duty," a "due concern for the welfare of our own souls and bodies." The command is that "we love our neighbour as ourselves, as truly and sincerely as we love ourselves," doing good to all, and in many cases denying ourselves "for the good of our neighbour." The grammatical-historical, Reformed and Wesleyan voices keep the plain weight: love to neighbour is the sum of the second table, flowing from and evidencing love to God, "for a right love to our brother, whom we have seen, is both an instance and an evidence of our love to God, whom we have not seen" (1 John 4:20).
40:
"On these two commandments hang all the law and the prophets." The witnesses read this as Christ's declaration that the whole of revealed duty depends upon love. The Latin Augustine fixes the sense of "hang": it means "refer thither as their end." Since there are two commandments, the love of God and of neighbour, Scripture sometimes names one for both — sometimes the love of God (Romans 8:28), sometimes the love of neighbour (Galatians 5:14) — "because if a man love his neighbour, it follows that he loves God also; for it is the selfsame affection by which we love God and by which we love our neighbour, save that we love God for Himself, but ourselves and our neighbour for God's sake." Yet "since the Divine substance is higher than our nature, the command to love God is distinct from that to love our neighbour."

The Latin Raban gives the structural reading that became standard: "to these two commandments belongs the whole decalogue; the commandments of the first table to the love of God, those of the second to the love of our neighbour." Hilary adds that on these two "hang all the Law and the Prophets," the obligation and merit of both being alike.

The Puritan Henry expands the thought devotionally: "all obedience begins in the affections, and nothing in religion is done right that is not done there first"; love is "the leading affection," and "all the law is fulfilled in one word, and that is, love" (Romans 13:10). He calls love "a short and sweet word; and, if that be the fulfilling of the law, surely the yoke of the command is very easy." The Reformed Calvin, the grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott, and the Wesleyan Wesley concur that the two commandments are "the spring and foundation of all the rest," not great by excluding others but "great because inclusive of others" (Henry). So the whole moral teaching of Scripture is gathered into a single root: love to God and love to neighbour, on which Law and Prophets alike depend. Gill adds that 'all the law and the prophets' are made to depend upon these two as the whole upon its foundation, so that whoever has love to God and neighbour has the substance of all that Moses and the prophets required; the two commandments are not a part of the Law but its very root and sum.
41:
"While the Pharisees were gathered together, Jesus asked them." The witnesses mark the turning of the tables: having parried every assault, Christ now becomes the questioner. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes that Mark and Luke place here the remark Matthew gives in v.46 — that "no man dared ask Him any more questions" — so that "from this time forth they have recourse to measures of another kind, and fall back upon treachery and false witness. It was now His turn to appear as the questioner, and to convict the Pharisees of resting on the mere surface even of the predictions which they quoted most confidently as Messianic."

The grammatical-historical Barnes states the threefold purpose: not "for vain parade and triumph," but "to show them how ignorant they were of their prophecies, to humble them in view of their ignorance, and to bring to their attention the true doctrine respecting the Messiah — his being possessed of a character superior to that of David... being his Lord, at the same time that he was his descendant." Clarke adds that Christ asks "utterly to confound them, and to show the people that the source of all their captious questions was their ignorance of the prophecies relative to the Messiah."

The Puritan Henry frames the reversal with relish: "they had put questions to him, one after another, out of the law; but he comes and puts a question to them upon the promise. Many are so full of the law that they forget Christ." He reads the moment as the confounding of the proud — "associate yourselves, and you shall be broken in pieces" (Isaiah 8:9). The Reformed Calvin and the Wesleyan Wesley keep to the design: Christ, "as the hour of his death was approaching, intended to attest his divinity, that all the godly might boldly rely on him" — for the question He is about to pose will prove the Messiah to be more than David's son. So the verse opens the last exchange of the day, in which the silenced tempters become, for once, the examined. Gill notes that Christ asks the question 'while the Pharisees were gathered together,' in the hearing of the people, so that their inability to answer might be public; the very assembly they had formed to entrap him becomes the audience of their own confounding, and the silenced tempters are, for once, the examined.
42:
"What think ye of Christ? whose son is he? They say unto him, The son of David." The witnesses treat the question as both a test of the Pharisees' Messianic doctrine and a probe into the deepest truth of Christ's person. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that "the Christ" means the Messiah, not Christ as a proper name; Robertson observes that "by his pungent question about the Messiah as David's son and Lord he really touches the problem of his Person — his Deity and his Humanity," a problem "the Pharisees had probably never faced before." Ellicott notes the freshness the question gained from the recent Hosannas, in which the crowds had hailed Jesus as "the son of David" (Matthew 21:9), and that the Pharisees give "the traditional answer" without ever having asked "whether it conveyed the whole truth."

Clarke draws an apologetic point from the answer: that the Messiah's descent from David "was a thing well known among the Jews and universally acknowledged" (John 7:42), yet now "their families are so confounded that they cannot trace any of their genealogies"; the prophecy that Messiah should come of David's line could only be verified "at a time when the public registers might be inspected" — which the evangelists did (Matthew 1; Luke 3), and which no scribe ever attempted to invalidate.

The Puritan Henry turns the question inward: "What think ye of Christ? It concerns each of us seriously to ask ourselves" — "some think not of him at all... but to them that believe he is precious." The Reformed Calvin diagnoses the error the Pharisees held: they expected a Redeemer who would be merely one of David's "sons and successors," bringing "nothing more elevated than human nature"; Satan, unable to deny Christ's humanity, "stripped him of his Divinity, as if he had been only one of the ordinary descendants of Adam," and so "the hope of eternal life was abolished." The verse thus opens the way to the proof that the Messiah is David's Lord as well as David's son. Gill observes that Christ does not ask whether there should be such a person — that the Pharisees did not doubt — but what they thought of him, 'as about his person, whether they thought him to be divine, or human,' and 'particularly what thoughts they had of his sonship, and who was his father'; the answer 'son of David,' true but partial, sets up the deeper question to come.
43:
"He saith unto them, How then doth David in spirit call him Lord, saying...?" The witnesses expound the force of the question and the authority of the text it rests on. The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets out its assumptions: that David wrote Psalm 110, that "in writing it he was guided by a Spirit higher than his own," and that "the subject of it was no earthly king of the house of David, but the far-off Christ" — a reading on which "there was an undisturbed consensus among the schools of Judaism," and which the apostles took up (Acts 2:34; Colossians 3:1; Hebrews 1:3). Barnes and Clarke note that "in spirit" means "by the inspiration of the Holy Spirit," David speaking "as a prophet" (Acts 2:30; 2 Samuel 23:2).

The Reformed Calvin meets the Jewish evasion that the psalm celebrates David himself: Christ "began with stating that the psalm was not composed in reference to the person of David, but was dictated prophetically to describe the future reign of Christ," as the psalm's own language shows, introducing "a king clothed with a new priesthood, by which the ancient shadows of the Law must be abolished" (Psalm 110:4). The argument is that David, "who was king and head of the people," calls this one Lord, "from which it follows that there is something in him greater than man."

The Puritan Henry presses the point: "it is easy to see that David calls Christ Lord, and this in spirit, being divinely inspired" — for "no man can say that Jesus is the Lord, but by the Holy Ghost" (1 Corinthians 12:3); but "it is not so easy for those who believe not the Godhead of the Messiah to clear this from an absurdity, if Christ be David's son," since "it is incongruous for the father to speak of his son... as his Lord." Cyril of Jerusalem cites the same text as the Saviour's own confirmation that David "spoke not these things of himself, but from the inspiration of the Holy Ghost." So the question is no idle riddle but a designed instruction "in a truth they were loth to believe — that the expected Messiah is God" (Henry).
44:
"The Lord said unto my Lord, Sit thou on my right hand, till I make thine enemies thy footstool." The witnesses expound the cited words of Psalm 110:1, distinguishing the two "Lords" and weighing the exaltation they describe. The grammatical-historical Clarke makes the philological point central to the argument: "The Lord (Jehovah) said unto my Lord (Adoni... my master, support)" — the first the divine name, the second a title of dominion — so that "David wrote it by the inspiration of God" and "it is a prophetic declaration of the Messiah." Barnes and Gill agree that "Jehovah said unto my Lord, the Messiah," David's "Adon" both by creation and redemption.

On "sit thou on my right hand," the witnesses read the highest exaltation. Barnes notes that "to be raised to the right hand of a king was significant of favour, trust, and power," fulfilled in Christ (Mark 16:19; Acts 7:55; Hebrews 1:3), and that the enemies made a "footstool" signify "all his spiritual foes" put in subjection (1 Corinthians 15:25; Hebrews 10:13). The Reformed Calvin reads the verse as "a song of triumph" given to all the godly, that "whatever may be the madness of men, all that they shall dare to contrive will be of no avail for destroying the kingdom of Christ, which has been set up by the appointment of God." The sitting at the right hand "does not denote any particular place, but embraces heaven and earth under the government of Christ"; and the subduing of enemies means that "his kingdom will remain invincible," his power "for ever unimpaired."

The Wesleyan Wesley sums it: "his dominion, to which David himself was subject, shows both the heavenly majesty of the king and the nature of his kingdom"; to sit at the right hand is "to remain in the highest authority and power." Cyril of Jerusalem gathers the apostolic witnesses to the session at God's right hand (Acts 2:34; Romans 8:34; Ephesians 1:20; Colossians 3:1; Hebrews 1:3). So the verse furnishes the premise of the unanswerable question: David's own Lord is enthroned at Jehovah's right hand, a dignity no mere descendant could claim.
45:
"If David then call him Lord, how is he his son?" The witnesses read this as the point of the whole exchange, the question that exposes the inadequacy of a merely human Messiah. The grammatical-historical Barnes states the logic: "if he was, then, David's Lord; if he was his superior; if he had an existence at that time, how could he be descended from him? They could not answer him." The only resolution, he adds, is "the admission that the Messiah was Divine as well as human — that he had an existence at the time of David, and was his Lord and Master, his God and King, and that as man he was descended from him."

Clarke notes the strategic completeness of the argument: "the Messiah is not only the son of David according to the flesh, but he is the Lord of David according to his Divine nature." Since the Jews "did not attempt to deny the conclusion," nor "deny that the prophecy related to Christ" — for it "was so fully and generally understood to belong to the Messiah" — they were "completely nonplussed and confounded."

The Puritan Henry resolves the seeming contradiction that the question raises: "whatever is said of Christ's humanity and humiliation must be construed in consistency with the truth of his divine nature and dominion. We must hold this fast, that he is David's Lord, and by that explain his being David's son." He cites the risen Christ's own words as the key the Pharisees lacked: "I am the root and the offspring of David" (Revelation 22:16) — "Christ, as God, was David's Root; Christ, as Man, was David's Offspring." So the difficulty the witnesses agree dissolves only one way: the Messiah is at once David's Lord by his eternal deity and David's son by his incarnation, and "if we hold not fast this truth, that Jesus Christ is over all God blessed for ever, we run ourselves into inextricable difficulties." The question that confounded the Pharisees becomes, for faith, the confession of the two natures in the one Christ. Gill notes that the Pharisees 'could not deny' that the Messiah was David's son, nor that David called him Lord, and yet could not reconcile the two; the reconciliation the witnesses press is the one they would not own — that the same Christ is David's Lord by his eternal Godhead and David's son by his taking of flesh.
46:
"And no man was able to answer him a word, neither durst any man from that day forth ask him any more questions." The witnesses read the close of the day's debates as both a silencing and a sober warning. The Puritan Henry gives the fullest reflection. The question "puzzled them": "either it was their ignorance that they did not know, or their impiety that they would not own, the Messiah to be God; which truth was the only key to unlock this difficulty." What the rabbis could not answer, he notes, "the plainest Christian that is led into the gospel can now account for: that Christ, as God, was David's Lord, and Christ, as Man, was David's son."

Henry then weighs the silence itself. It "silenced them, and all others that sought occasion against him," so that "neither durst any man ask him any more such captious questions." But he draws a searching lesson: "God will glorify himself in the silencing of many whom he will not glorify himself in the salvation of. Many are convinced that are not converted by the word." Had these been converted, "they would have asked him more questions, especially that great question, What must we do to be saved? But since they could not gain their point, they would have no more to do with him."

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices read the verse as the narrative's verdict on the whole sequence of assaults: Pharisees, Herodians, Sadducees, and lawyer have each in turn been confounded, and now "from that day forth" the open contest ceases. Gill, Barnes and Clarke note that the cessation of questions marks the completeness of Christ's victory in argument, after which his enemies turn from debate to plot. So the chapter that opened with the parable of the slighted feast closes with the questioners themselves reduced to silence — convinced but unconverted, unable to answer and unwilling to believe, withdrawing from the One whom they could neither trap nor receive. Gill, Barnes and Clarke note that this silence 'from that day forth' marks the close of the open contest: every party having been confounded, the enemies of Christ turn from questioning to plotting, and the chapter that began with the parable of the slighted feast ends with the tempters themselves reduced to a silence they could neither break nor escape.
